Calculated compliance ellipse."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="F0004" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div><p></p></div><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_2" id="S003-S2002"><h3 class="section-heading-3" id="d1e1455">3.2.  Endpoint compliance measurement experiment</h3><p>We conducted an endpoint compliance measurement experiment in horizontal posture to verify the validity of the previous section. The experiment was conducted by stopping the base motors with Super Dragon maintained horizontally. As shown in Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0005" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">5</button>, the experiment was conducted by connecting a force gauge (Nidec-Shimpo: FGP-50) to the Super Dragon's tip with a rope, and measuring the tension when pulling and releasing in both positive and negative directions along the <i>y</i>-axis and <i>z</i>-axis. The tip position was measured by driving the force gauge at a constant speed using a linear stage (COMS: PM40B-25X) and reading the tension at regular time intervals, measuring the tip position twice in each of the four directions along the <i>y</i> and <i>z</i> axes. Here, since rope elongation occurs due to pulling the arm tip and the passage of time, increasing the joint angle error from the horizontal posture, we corrected the joint angles to horizontal posture after two experiments in each direction. The movement speed of the linear stage was <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/a82ec56c-63d2-418c-b2bb-8ce1efd1fe3f/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0048.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mn>1</mn><mspace width="thinmathspace"></mspace><mrow><mtext>mm/s</mtext></mrow></math></span> for the first measurement in the <i>y</i>-axis positive direction and <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/eb388853-ea01-4f97-948d-ccde07558b50/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0049.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mn>2</mn><mspace width="thinmathspace"></mspace><mrow><mtext>mm/s</mtext></mrow></math></span> for all others.
</p><div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure 5. </span></span> Compliance measurement experiment (<i>y</i>-axis positive direction).</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="F0005"><img id="d1e1509" src="/cms/asset/5641dcd7-01e0-4c7f-9f78-eddc3bbc4b0a/tadr_a_2642632_f0005_oc.jpg" loading="lazy" height="329" width="500" alt="Figure 5. Compliance measurement experiment (y-axis positive direction)."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="F0005" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div><p></p><p>The measurement results from the rise of tension to maximum tension were taken as the measurement results. Next, the measurement results were linearly approximated using the least squares method (<span class="monospace">polyfit</span> function, Matlab). The data are displayed such that the line for pulling passes through the origin, and the line for releasing intersects at the position of maximum tension (Figures <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0006" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">6</button> and <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0007" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">7</button>). Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0006" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">6</button> shows the measurement results in the <i>y</i>-axis positive direction and <i>y</i>-axis negative direction from top to bottom, with the first and second measurements shown from left to right for each direction. Similarly, Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0007" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">7</button> shows the measurement results in the <i>z</i>-axis direction. The average value of the measured slopes in each data set was taken as the tip stiffness. By taking the reciprocal, the compliance in the <i>y</i> and <i>z</i>-axis directions was calculated as 31.8 and <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/c31dccc8-7a86-452f-896f-fd0e404f7644/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0050.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mn>22.6</mn><mspace width="thinmathspace"></mspace><mrow><mtext>mm/N</mtext></mrow></math></span>, respectively.
</p><div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure 6. </span></span> Result of compliance measurement in <i>y</i>-direction. The measurement data obtained from the force gauge are displayed as scatter plots. The dashed lines indicate approximate lines obtained by linearly approximating the measurement data.</p><p class="captionText">Left side: 1st measurement. Right side: 2nd measurement.</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="F0006"><img id="d1e1565" src="/cms/asset/d5ab35e3-77e1-4764-b030-6ad4f34adce7/tadr_a_2642632_f0006_oc.jpg" loading="lazy" height="500" width="486" alt="Figure 6. The dashed lines indicate approximate lines obtained by linearly approximating the measurement data.Left side: 1st measurement. Right side: 2nd measurement."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="F0006" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div>
<div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure 7. </span></span> Result of compliance measurement in <i>z</i>-direction.The measurement data obtained from the force gauge are displayed as scatter plots. The dashed lines indicate approximate lines obtained by linearly approximating the measurement data.</p><p class="captionText">Left side: 1st measurement. Right side: 2nd measurement.</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="F0007"><img id="d1e1579" src="/cms/asset/347b6af6-9c6a-4c65-9aa9-b501775289dd/tadr_a_2642632_f0007_oc.jpg" loading="lazy" height="500" width="493" alt="Figure 7. Result of compliance measurement in z-direction.The measurement data obtained from the force gauge are displayed as scatter plots. The dashed lines indicate approximate lines obtained by linearly approximating the measurement data.Left side: 1st measurement. Right side: 2nd measurement."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="F0007" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div><p></p><p>We compare the joint angle changes when pulled 0.2 m in the first measurements in the <i>y</i>-axis positive direction and <i>z</i>-axis positive direction with the analytical results. The analytical calculation method for joint angles uses the pseudo-inverse matrix of the Jacobian matrix derived as shown in Section <a href="#S003-S2001" data-behaviour="scroll" data-behaviour-ref="#S003-S2001" data-behaviour-callback="focus" data-behaviour-callback-target="#S003-S2001">3.1</a> for Equation (<span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref disp-formula"><a data-rid="M0005" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="disp-formula" data-label="equation"><span class="off-screen">Equation</span>5</a></span><span class="hidden" id="M0005"><span class="NLM_disp-formula-image disp-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/ef1dea41-e9a5-46d4-85d1-14e0b8a64081/tadr_a_2642632_m0005.gif"}"><span class="mml-formula"><span class="disp_formula_label_div">(5) </span></span></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula disp-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mtable columnalign="right left right left right left right left right left right left" columnspacing="0em 2em 0em 2em 0em 2em 0em 2em 0em 2em 0em" displaystyle="true" rowspacing="3pt"><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">x</mi><mrow><mtext>e</mtext></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">J</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">θ</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></math><span class="mathjaxLabel"><span class="disp_formula_label_div">(5) </span></span></span></span>) to calculate the joint angle changes when giving 0.2 m displacement in the <i>y</i> and <i>z</i>-axis directions, respectively. The measured values and analytical results are shown in Table <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="T0001" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="table">1</button>.
</p><div class="tableView"><div class="tableCaption"><div class="short-legend"><h3><p class="captionText"><span class="captionLabel">Table 1. </span> Joint angle changes and analytical results in compliance measurement experiments.</p></h3></div></div><div class="tableDownloadOption" id="T0001-table-wrapper"><a data-id="T0001" class="downloadButton btn btn-sm displaySizeTable" href="#" role="button" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="table">Display Table</a></div></div><p></p><p class="last">We compare these results with the analytical results from the previous section. The intersection point on the horizontal axis (labeled <i>y</i>-axis compliance) of the endpoint compliance ellipsoid shown in Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0004" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">4</button> indicates the compliance when force is applied laterally to the arm tip. Similarly, the compliance in the <i>z</i>-axis direction can be obtained, with the compliance in the <i>y</i>-axis and <i>z</i>-axis directions being 315 and <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/74753928-e135-44a8-86c8-85b95c47fb1a/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0061.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mn>234</mn><mspace width="thinmathspace"></mspace><mrow><mtext>mm/N</mtext></mrow></math></span>, respectively. These results are shown in Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0008" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">8</button>. The blue bars represent the analytical values of endpoint compliance obtained in Section <a href="#S003-S2001" data-behaviour="scroll" data-behaviour-ref="#S003-S2001" data-behaviour-callback="focus" data-behaviour-callback-target="#S003-S2001">3.1</a>, and the red bars represent the measured values obtained from measurement experiments. In contrast, as shown in Section <a href="#S003-S2003" data-behaviour="scroll" data-behaviour-ref="#S003-S2003" data-behaviour-callback="focus" data-behaviour-callback-target="#S003-S2003">3.3</a>, the analytical values of endpoint compliance obtained using the equivalent tendon stiffness <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/1c474205-ff31-4e96-a91d-a8723df924b3/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0062.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">K</mi><mrow><mtext>exp</mtext></mrow></msub></math></span> including frictional forces acting on the rope are shown in green. The measured values compared to the analytical solutions were <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/d84497ef-0045-450b-b83a-711c6dc5697f/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0063.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mn>9.91</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi></math></span> in the <i>y</i>-axis direction and <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/9dfc47b4-76d1-4668-a815-d67fd8f018cb/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0064.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mn>10.4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi></math></span> in the <i>z</i>-axis direction, showing lower compliance in the measured values but confirming that the order of magnitude matches.
</p><div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure 8. </span></span> The analytical solution and measured values of endpoint compliance in the <i>y</i>-axis and <i>z</i>-axis. Blue: The analytical solution using <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/91eea01b-7852-4df4-afb5-cbbc5a8c7cdf/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0065.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">K</mi><mrow><mtext>sim</mtext></mrow></msub></math></span>. Green: The analytical solution using <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/c08b3be8-a2f3-447b-b54c-a795c40f8fca/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0066.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">K</mi><mrow><mtext>exp</mtext></mrow></msub></math></span>. Red: Measured values.</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="F0008"><img id="d1e1943" src="/cms/asset/68849124-d5fb-4463-be31-5d0b3dd8d76a/tadr_a_2642632_f0008_oc.jpg" loading="lazy" height="332" width="500" alt="Figure 8. The analytical solution and measured values of endpoint compliance in the y-axis and z-axis. Blue: The analytical solution using Ksim. Green: The analytical solution using Kexp. Red: Measured values."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="F0008" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div><p></p></div><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_2" id="S003-S2003"><h3 class="section-heading-3" id="d1e1946">3.3.  Consideration</h3><p>We discuss the results from the previous section. As shown in Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0008" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">8</button>, the measured endpoint compliance values are approximately 10% of the analytically obtained values. The cause of such numerical differences is considered to be the influence of frictional forces acting on the joint control tendons. In Section <a href="#S003-S2001" data-behaviour="scroll" data-behaviour-ref="#S003-S2001" data-behaviour-callback="focus" data-behaviour-callback-target="#S003-S2001">3.1</a>, the analysis was performed under the assumption that tension is uniformly distributed along the rope with respect to elongation. However, in reality, the rope tension is not uniformly distributed due to rotational friction at pulleys where the rope is wound around and frictional forces arising from contact with the rope itself at sections wound around pulleys. The non-uniform distribution of rope tension may have resulted in the measured values showing smaller compliance. Here, we consider calculating the equivalent stiffness of the joint control tendons using joint angular displacement data and tension data obtained from logs in the endpoint compliance measurement experiment presented in Section <a href="#S003-S2002" data-behaviour="scroll" data-behaviour-ref="#S003-S2002" data-behaviour-callback="focus" data-behaviour-callback-target="#S003-S2002">3.2</a>. If higher tension than expected was acting due to frictional forces, the obtained joint control tendon stiffness represents an equivalent stiffness that includes the influence of friction. Therefore, we calculate the equivalent stiffness of each joint control tendon obtained from compliance measurements and re-analytically determine the endpoint compliance based on this to investigate the influence of frictional forces acting on the rope on endpoint compliance.</p><p>Here, we calculate the equivalent stiffness of joint control tendons based on joint angle and tension data from the compliance measurement experiment in the positive <i>z</i>-axis direction. The reason for focusing on <i>z</i>-axis direction measurements is that most of Super Dragon's joints are pitch joints, and more joint control tendons are expected to influence the results compared to the <i>y</i>-axis direction, thus yielding more accurate results. Since all the actuators were completely stopped, elongation <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/6ebd2b81-ebdd-4a40-8b80-daf0a5f6303b/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0067.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">l</mi></math></span> occurs in each joint control tendon solely due to joint angular displacement caused by pulling the arm's endpoint. The elongation <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/2bc72803-adc9-44e3-8f94-eed78bb609e4/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0068.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">l</mi></math></span> can be expressed as <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/b96d0819-5908-4140-a591-2794f7fcbfd7/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0069.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">l</mi><mo>=</mo><msubsup><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">R</mi><mrow><mtext>j</mtext></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">⊤</mi></mrow></msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">θ</mi></math></span> using joint angular displacement data <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/4bcdab84-6e6d-4872-8818-8a1a5c2ba509/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0070.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">θ</mi></math></span> and the pulley matrix <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/95c60b1b-e0f7-4e3d-9a0d-cffde82559d4/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0071.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">R</mi><mrow><mtext>j</mtext></mrow></msub></math></span>. Note that the elongation <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/c8e5c957-232c-4c27-b73b-f9f63f65b8a7/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0072.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">l</mi></math></span> here represents the elongation from the initial horizontal posture and differs from the elongation from the natural length of each joint control tendon. Linear interpolation was performed on the experimentally obtained elongation <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/f21d7331-b805-44c3-967d-5c332f251a57/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0073.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">l</mi></math></span> [m] and tension <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/49913bcd-604f-48ad-b7f6-103f01a232b0/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0074.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">f</mi></math></span> [N] data (<span class="monospace">polyfit</span> function, Matlab) to obtain an equivalent stiffness matrix including the effects of friction, denoted as <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/1291c643-df91-4aca-ac72-1567d1e57b41/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0075.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">K</mi><mrow><mtext>exp</mtext></mrow></msub></math></span> [N/m]. Here, multiplying the diagonal components of <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/449efc44-1b0c-4046-9093-f3776260d06a/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0076.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">K</mi><mrow><mtext>exp</mtext></mrow></msub></math></span> by the natural length of each rope yields the strain stiffness <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/bf06de00-d1b6-4035-9dc9-f8738e896f32/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0077.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">k</mi><mrow><mtext>exp</mtext></mrow></msub></math></span>. However, it is difficult to measure all unloaded tendon lengths in advance for Super Dragon, where numerous tendons are wound around pulleys. Additionally, when the arm is maintained in a horizontal posture, joint control tendons experience tensions up to approximately 1000 N, but the resulting elongation from the unloaded state is considered sufficiently small compared to rope lengths on the order of several meters. Therefore, in this paper, we multiply the equivalent stiffness <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/49c20edc-975a-4fe0-9838-7ebb321337bd/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0078.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">K</mi><mrow><mtext>exp</mtext></mrow></msub></math></span> of the joint control tendons by the rope length of the joint control tendons in the horizontal posture, and treat the obtained approximate values as the strain stiffness <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/57a25a4e-2a60-4d98-927c-a0816982c709/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0079.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">k</mi><mrow><mtext>exp</mtext></mrow></msub></math></span>.</p><p>Each component of the obtained <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/48f6f93f-e5ec-49cb-a14c-40c042f2ac21/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0080.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">k</mi><mrow><mtext>exp</mtext></mrow></msub></math></span> is shown in Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0009" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">9</button>. Rope numbers follow the convention where the two ropes driving the <i>n</i>-th joint are designated as ropes 2<i>n</i>−1 and <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/a9d350fd-de25-4e7e-bf42-a788ecf34ca6/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0081.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mn>2</mn><mi>n</mi></math></span>. Graphs for pitch joint control tendons are shown in magenta, and those for yaw joint control tendons in cyan. The strain stiffness value <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/77c25167-ab4f-44e8-a41b-f7c2aae0649a/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0082.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3.40</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mspace width="thinmathspace"></mspace><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow></math></span> used in the analysis in Section <a href="#S003-S2001" data-behaviour="scroll" data-behaviour-ref="#S003-S2001" data-behaviour-callback="focus" data-behaviour-callback-target="#S003-S2001">3.1</a> is indicated by a black dashed line. As can be seen from Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0009" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">9</button>, joint control tendons closer to the base show values closer to <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/38ecbce1-b9fa-4d18-9adf-b2d13405bd20/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0083.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3.40</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mspace width="thinmathspace"></mspace><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow></math></span>, while those on the tip side, which are wound around more pulleys, tend to show larger values. Thus, it was newly found that frictional forces acting on the rope increase measured tension values and contribute to increased equivalent stiffness.

data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div><p></p><p>Using the obtained <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/1d91eb10-5ab9-4825-93fc-34457a5fdc83/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0084.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula endpoint compliance using Equation (<span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref disp-formula"><a data-rid="M0007" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="disp-formula" data-label="equation"><span class="off-screen">Equation</span>7</a></span><span class="hidden" id="M0007"><span class="NLM_disp-formula-image disp-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/039231d8-ce13-42d1-bc8e-871b55cd83fb/tadr_a_2642632_m0007.gif"}"><span class="mml-formula"><span class="disp_formula_label_div">(7) mathvariant="bold-italic">J</mi><mrow><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></mtd></mtr></mtable></math><span class="mathjaxLabel"><span class="disp_formula_label_div">(7) </span></span></span></span>), similar to Section <a href="#S003-S2001" data-behaviour="scroll" data-behaviour-ref="#S003-S2001" data-behaviour-callback="focus" data-behaviour-callback-target="#S003-S2001">3.1</a>. Additionally, let <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/09586457-f70c-4630-b3c0-eaef5555d4b8/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0085.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">K</mi><mrow><mtext>sim</mtext></mrow></msub></math></span> denote the tendon stiffness matrix calculated from the strain stiffness <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/e6a58aac-07b4-4bbb-bd71-0d1e4ec1708d/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0086.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3.40</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mspace width="thinmathspace"></mspace><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow></math></span> obtained from viscoelastic measurements used in Section <a href="#S003-S2001" data-behaviour="scroll" data-behaviour-ref="#S003-S2001" data-behaviour-callback="focus" data-behaviour-callback-target="#S003-S2001">3.1</a>. The resulting ellipsoids are shown in Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0010" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">10</button>. The ellipsoid obtained using <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/5e90176d-a654-4a81-9fd0-9a8c21876afb/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0087.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">K</mi><mrow><mtext>sim</mtext></mrow></msub></math></span> is shown as a blue solid line, and that obtained using <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/485650ca-66f5-4f7a-a600-84b237305ee5/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0088.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">K</mi><mrow><mtext>exp</mtext></mrow></msub></math></span> as a green solid line. The measured endpoint compliance values obtained in Section <a href="#S003-S2002" data-behaviour="scroll" data-behaviour-ref="#S003-S2002" data-behaviour-callback="focus" data-behaviour-callback-target="#S003-S2002">3.2</a> are plotted as red points. As shown in Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0010" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">10</button>, using <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/aa33e62e-8cda-48c2-8bb6-e9cf29791dd4/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0089.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">K</mi><mrow><mtext>exp</mtext></mrow></msub></math></span> yields a smaller compliance ellipsoid. The obtained endpoint compliance values in the <i>y</i>-axis and <i>z</i>-axis directions are shown in green in Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0004" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">4</button>. As shown in Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0004" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">4</button>, while the analytical values using <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/d89c47cf-d4af-4022-b14b-dc89ba9ee915/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0090.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">K</mi><mrow><mtext>sim</mtext></mrow></msub></math></span> differ from the measured values by approximately an order of magnitude, the analytical values based on <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/9321a00d-e62d-4b25-a077-d09818615bf9/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0091.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">K</mi><mrow><mtext>exp</mtext></mrow></msub></math></span> remain within approximately three times the measured values. Regarding this approximately threefold difference, we believe it is due to factors that do not affect joint control tendon tension values, such as link deflection, minor play in mechanism assembly, and friction in bearings of joint mechanism components connecting links other than pulleys, as well as nonlinearity in tendon stiffness with respect to elongation. From the above, it was found that using equivalent tendon stiffness including the effects of frictional forces acting on the rope in endpoint compliance analysis enables obtaining analytical values closer to measured values.
</p><div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure 10. </span></span> Calculated endpoint compliance ellipse. Blue: The analytical solution using <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/7303bcb1-65fb-4aa0-8114-f27fe2279fc3/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0092.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">K</mi><mrow><mtext>sim</mtext></mrow></msub></math></span>. Green: The analytical solution using <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/d470b2db-34ab-45cd-a1af-ad8846301a21/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0093.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">K</mi><mrow><mtext>exp</mtext></mrow></msub></math></span>. Red: Measured values.</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="F0010"><img id="d1e2363" src="/cms/asset/885781d3-5a93-4e3d-b6cb-a56f413c66fc/tadr_a_2642632_f0010_oc.jpg" loading="lazy" height="449" width="500" alt="Figure 10. Calculated endpoint compliance ellipse. Blue: The analytical solution using Ksim. Green: The analytical solution using Kexp. Red: Measured values."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="F0010" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div><p></p><p>From Table <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="T0001" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="table">1</button>, the joint angle changes in the experiment where the arm tip was pulled in the <i>y</i>-axis positive direction show a maximum error of 0.3 deg compared to the analytical results, indicating good agreement. For the <i>z</i>-axis positive direction, the maximum error between measured values and analytical results was 1.0 deg. This is considered to be due to the influence of gravity, and although the error is larger compared to the <i>y</i>-axis direction, it mostly agrees with the analytical results. From the above, the analytical results are considered to be valid.</p><p class="last">Thus, we have shown through analysis and experiments that the coupled tendon-driven articulated arm Super Dragon can achieve compliance without special control due to the elongation of joint control tendons. Simultaneously, we revealed that analytically calculated endpoint compliance, when the rope becomes long, exhibits errors despite matching the order of magnitude with measured values due to the influence of frictional forces acting on the rope.</p></div></div><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_1" id="S004"><h2 class="section-heading-2" id="d1e2381">4.  Contact experiments with external environments</h2><p>Utilizing the compliance shown in the previous section, we conducted motion experiments involving contact with obstacles. We conducted two experiments: one where the arm tip contacts an obstacle, and another where part of the arm's gravity is supported by the external environment. Additionally, assuming that investigation equipment is mounted, a 5 kg weight is attached to the tip of Super Dragon.</p><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_2" id="S004-S2001"><h3 class="section-heading-3" id="d1e2386">4.1.  Contact experiment with horizontally positioned obstacles</h3><p class="last">As shown in Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0011" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">11</button>, we placed an obstacle on the right side as viewed from the base of the arm, and conducted a motion experiment where the arm tip contacts the obstacle and then separates. Here, the installed obstacle is a wooden rectangular parallelepiped, which has a structure sufficiently rigid relative to the force generated by the Super Dragon's endpoint. The data of joint angle measurements and joint torques calculated from tension measurements using Equation (<span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref disp-formula"><a data-rid="M0002" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="disp-formula" data-label="equation"><span class="off-screen">Equation</span>2</a></span><span class="hidden" id="M0002"><span class="NLM_disp-formula-image disp-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/a4eb04ce-337c-4629-90e4-0864a6aca8be/tadr_a_2642632_m0002.gif"}"><span class="mml-formula"><span class="disp_formula_label_div">(2) </span></span></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula disp-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mtable columnalign="right left right left right left right left right left right left" columnspacing="0em 2em 0em 2em 0em 2em 0em 2em 0em 2em 0em" displaystyle="true" rowspacing="3pt"><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">τ</mi><mo>=</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">Rf</mi></mrow><mo>=</mo><mrow><mo>[</mo><mtable columnspacing="1em" rowspacing="4pt"><mtr><mtd><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">R</mi><mrow><mtext>j</mtext></mrow></msub><mspace width="thinmathspace"></mspace><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">R</mi><mrow><mtext>wc</mtext></mrow></msub></mtd></mtr></mtable><mo>]</mo></mrow><mrow><mo>[</mo><mtable columnspacing="1em" rowspacing="4pt"><mtr><mtd><msub><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">f</mi><mrow><mtext>j</mtext></mrow></msub></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><msub><mi>f</mi><mrow><mtext>wc</mtext></mrow></msub></mtd></mtr></mtable><mo>]</mo></mrow></mtd></mtr></mtable></math><span class="mathjaxLabel"><span class="disp_formula_label_div">(2) </span></span></span></span>) during the experiment are shown in Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0012" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">12</button>. Note that in Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0012" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">12</button>(a), the data for each joint angle are plotted with solid lines, while the target value for 6th joint is plotted with a dashed line. The arm tip contacted the obstacle at 134 s and separated from the obstacle at 153 s. During this motion, no hardware damage was observed to either the arm or the obstacle. From Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0012" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">12</button>(c), the absolute values of joint torques for the yaw joints (6th, 8th, and 10th joints) increased during contact compared to before contact, due to the arm tip receiving external force in the <i>y</i>-axis direction. It is considered that contact between the arm and the external environment can be detected by the operator measuring these changes in joint torque. In this way, we achieved contact with the external environment without damage by utilizing the compliance of the coupled tendon-driven articulated arm, and demonstrated the possibility of detecting such contact.
</p><div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure 11. </span></span> The arm tip in contact with the wall.</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="F0011"><img id="d1e2417" src="/cms/asset/1cffb242-33f6-4c24-af04-7fdebe3bfaaf/tadr_a_2642632_f0011_oc.jpg" loading="lazy" height="325" width="500" alt="Figure 11. The arm tip in contact with the wall."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="F0011" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div>
<div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure 12. </span></span> The results of when the arm tip comes into contact with the wall.</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="F0012"><img id="d1e2427" src="/cms/asset/d31e79fb-2c7e-4b66-83bb-bf6bb14f337c/tadr_a_2642632_f0012_oc.jpg" loading="lazy" height="412" width="500" alt="Figure 12. The results of when the arm tip comes into contact with the wall."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="F0012" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div><p></p></div><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_2" id="S004-S2002"><h3 class="section-heading-3" id="d1e2430">4.2.  Joint torque reduction through contact with the external environment</h3><p class="last">In this section, we attempted to reduce joint torque through contact between the arm and the external environment. In other words, rather than detecting and avoiding contact with the external environment, we actively exploit such contact. The required joint torque is determined by the arm's posture and tip payload, which in turn determines the target values for driving tendon tension. However, in practice, there are limits to the tendon strength, which constrains the joint torque that can be generated at each joint. Therefore, postures requiring high joint torque are difficult to maintain, and the workspace is limited. Here, the workspace is defined as the set of positions reachable by the end effector in three-dimensional space. Therefore, by utilizing contact with the external environment to reduce the joint torque required for posture maintenance, tendon tension can also be reduced, potentially resulting in expanding workspace. As shown in Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0013" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">13</button>, we conducted an experiment where the 9th joint (pitch) was brought into contact with an obstacle placed on the ground from above, and then the 10th joint (yaw) was driven to swing the tip left and right. Note that in this experiment, since it is sufficient to obtain a reaction force from the floor, we determined that the obstacle does not need to be rigid and used a cushioned air bed. The joint angle progression and joint torques during this time are shown in Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0014" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">14</button>. The joint torque calculation method is the same as in the previous section. Note that in Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0014" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">14</button>(a), the data for each joint angle are plotted with solid lines, while the target value for 9th joint and 10th joint is plotted with a dashed line. The horizontal axis in Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0014" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">14</button> shows time, and the 9th joint and obstacle were in contact from 379 to 529 s. As shown in the lower part of Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0014" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">14</button>, the absolute values of joint torques were reduced during this interval. This is because the 9th joint receives an upward reaction force from contacting the obstacle, supporting part of the gravity. Additionally, as shown in Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0014" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">14</button>, we successfully drove the 10th joint by <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/cf46e672-fd7c-45b5-b8ca-035eee21fd00/tadr_a_2642632_ilm0094.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mo>±</mo><mn>10</mn><mspace width="thinmathspace"></mspace><mrow><mtext>deg</mtext></mrow></math></span> while in contact with the external environment from 403 to 496 s. Thus, we found that joints can be driven while reducing joint torque by utilizing external contact. This makes it possible to significantly expand the arm's workspace.
</p><div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure 13. </span></span> Experiment on reducing joint torque by utilizing the external environment.</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="F0013"><img id="d1e2472" src="/cms/asset/f9ccd915-2108-4c6a-8fef-87c6017efcec/tadr_a_2642632_f0013_oc.jpg" loading="lazy" height="349" width="500" alt="Figure 13. Experiment on reducing joint torque by utilizing the external environment."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="F0013" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div>
<div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure 14. </span></span> The results of the experiment on reducing joint torque by utilizing the external environment.</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="F0014"><img id="d1e2482" src="/cms/asset/e2123c7c-8892-4e85-8d50-115b5915c6db/tadr_a_2642632_f0014_oc.jpg" loading="lazy" height="500" width="434" alt="Figure 14. The results of the experiment on reducing joint torque by utilizing the external environment."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="F0014" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div><p></p></div></div><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_1" id="S005"><h2 class="section-heading-2" id="d1e2485">5.  Conclusion</h2><p>In this paper, we analytically demonstrated that the coupled tendon-driven articulated arm Super Dragon has compliance due to the elasticity of joint control tendons, and confirmed this using actual hardware. Furthermore, utilizing this compliance, we showed that the arm can safely contact the external environment, and demonstrated the possibility of detecting external contact through changes in torque values calculated from tension measurements. Moreover, we experimentally demonstrated that joint torque can be reduced by intentionally contacting the external environment rather than detecting and avoiding such contact. In this experiment, we successfully reduced the required joint torque magnitude of each joint by contacting the external environment and successfully operated the tip joint in that state.</p><p class="last">As future work, we plan to derive a calculation method to identify the contact point position with the arm from changes in torque values during external contact, and conduct principle verification using actual hardware.</p></div><div class="pb-dropzone" data-pb-dropzone="scholixLinksDropZone"><!-- Empty dropzone --></div><div id="coi-statement" class="NLM_sec"><h2 class="section-heading-2" id="d1e2492">Disclosure statement</h2><p class="last">No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).</p></div></div><script Measurement of the elastic coefficient of joint control tendons </h3><p>This section describes measurements conducted as part of the analysis of Super Dragon's endpoint compliance.</p><p>In a prior study of the analysis of the elastic modulus of synthetic fiber ropes, Takata et al. [<span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0015" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>15</a></span>] clarified the elongation characteristics of several types of synthetic fiber ropes. Specifically, they confirmed the applicability of the model proposed by Flory et al. [<span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0016" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>16</a></span>] to thin synthetic fiber ropes with diameters of approximately 2 mm, such as the joint control tendons of Super Dragon, and then derived viscoelastic parameters based on this model. Flory's model represents the nonlinear elastoplasticity of marine synthetic fiber ropes using a four-element model and a ratchet and spring (Figure <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0015" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">A1</button>). The four-element model consists of a spring representing elongation when the loading rate is sufficiently fast, a dashpot representing long-term creep deformation, and a spring-dashpot element representing elongation when the loading rate is sufficiently slow, while the ratchet represents plastic deformation.</p><div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure A1. </span></span> Flory's model [<span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0016" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>16</a></span>].</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="F0015"><img id="d1e3003" src="/cms/asset/e8957c45-9ea3-4d16-a484-7642f3729b12/tadr_a_2642632_f0015_oc.jpg" loading="lazy" height="214" width="500" alt="Figure A1. Flory's model [Citation16]."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="F0015" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div><p>Assuming this model, when tension is instantaneously applied to a synthetic fiber rope specimen at time <i>t</i> = 0 and kept constant, the tension value <span class="mathjaxLabel"><span class="disp_formula_label_div">(A4) </span></span></span>The measurements were performed using a tensile testing machine (AG-I, Shimadzu Co.), with the rope specimen secured in grips spaced 100 mm apart. The specimen was then tensioned with 1000 N, and the grips repositioned to release the load to a point where the specimen would not slacken (below 5 N), and this state was maintained for 10 min. Finally, the specimen was pulled 1.6 mm and maintained for 10 min while recording tension data.</p><p>For the obtained tension data, we used the simplex method (<span class="monospace">fminsearch</span> function, Matlab) to determine the elastic and viscous coefficients (<span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt=""                 <label for="harvardRadio"><input type="radio" value="harvard" id="harvardRadio" name="referenceStyle">Harvard</label>

            </div>

                <p id="csl-response" class="csl-response copy__text" tabindex="-1"></p>
                <div class="copy-section">

                        <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 512 512" fill="green" width="12" height="12">
                            <path d="M256 512A256 256 0 1 0 256 0a256 256 0 1 0 0 512zM369 209L241 337c-9.4 9.4-24.6 9.4-33.9 0l-64-64c-9.4-9.4-9.4-24.6 0-33.9s24.6-9.4 33.9 0l47 47L335 175c9.4-9.4 24.6-9.4 33.9 0s9.4 24.6 0 33.9z"></path>
                        </svg>

                    </div>

                    </button>
                </div>
            </div>

        </div>

            <div>Download a citation file in RIS format that can be imported by citation management software including

            </div>

                <label for="risRadio"><input type="radio" value="ris" id="risRadio" name="format" checked="checked">RIS</label>

                <span id="chooseOption">Choose options:</span>
                <label for="citRadio"><input type="radio" value="cit" id="citRadio" name="include" checked="checked">Citation</label>

                <label for="refRadio"><input type="radio" value="ref" id="refRadio" name="include">Citation & references</label>
            </div>
        </div>

                <i class="fa fa-download" aria-hidden="true"></i>
                Download citations
            </button></div>
        </div>
        <input name="doi" type="hidden" value="10.1080/01691864.2026.2642632" tabindex="-1">
