gold_text: Endpoint compliance in coupled tendon-driven long-reach articulated arm 'Super Dragon' ABSTRACT For decommissioning work at the Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Station, internal investigation using long-reach articulated arms is required. Inside the reactor, it is difficult to completely understand the situation, in advance avoid contact with obstacles. The coupled tendon-driven articulated arm 'Super Dragon' can achieve high endpoint compliance without special control thanks to the elasticity of synthetic fiber ropes used for driving, making it highly adaptable to unknown environments for investigation purposes. This paper aims to demonstrate the high environmental adaptability of coupled tendon-driven arms. We showed the arm's endpoint compliance through numerical analysis and hardware experiments. Furthermore, leveraging this compliance, we achieved external contact detection using measured tension and joint torque decrease. GRAPHICAL ABSTRACT Keywords: Long-reach articulated arm, coupled tendon drive, endpoint compliance, synthetic fiber rope 1. Introduction For the decommissioning work of the Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Station, internal investigation of the Primary Containment Vessel (PCV) is required. To achieve internal investigation of the PCV, which is a high-radiation area, an investigation method has been proposed where an articulated arm equipped with investigation equipment at the tip penetrates into the PCV for exploration, and various articulated arms have been developed [Citation1-5]. In this method, the arm is required to be long-reach and slim to pass through narrow spaces and investigate vast areas, as well as have a large payload to carry investigation equipment. We believe that adaptability to unknown environments is necessary in addition to these conditions. While typical robotic arm exploration work requires precise control to avoid obstacles, in PCV investigation, it is difficult to avoid contact with the external environment as the internal situation cannot be grasped in advance. Therefore, we consider that an articulated arm with a certain degree of compliance would be useful to prevent damage when the arm contacts the external environment. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are no prior studies on articulated arms for decommissioning work that focus on adaptability during external contact. We have developed Super Dragon, a coupled tendon-driven super long-reach articulated arm [Citation6-10]. Generally, as robot arms become longer, their mass increases, making it difficult to develop arms that are both long-reach and slim. However, coupled tendon drive can achieve long-reach, lightweight, and large payload by placing actuators at the base and supporting the root joints, which apply the largest gravity torque, through coupled drive [Citation11]. In this paper, in addition to these advantages, we focus on the feature that compliance can be achieved without special control due to the elasticity of the synthetic fiber ropes used for driving. In the literature, Series Elastic Actuators (SEAs) intentionally incorporate elastic elements into actuators to achieve compliance through mechanical elasticity rather than active actuator control [Citation12]. SEAs improve impact resistance by low-pass filtering impact loads through their elastic elements, and this effectiveness has been verified using a geared motor incorporating a torsion spring as the elastic element. In our case, we believe that long-path synthetic fiber ropes, such as those used in this study, can also serve as elastic elements. In other words, it is considered possible to allow contact between the arm itself and the external environment while penetrating without destroying the arm. Furthermore, since the base is equipped with tension sensors for the driving tendons, we believe that contact with the external environments can be detected without special equipment by utilizing the change in tension during external contact. Moreover, if contact between the arm and the external environment is allowed, it is considered possible to reduce joint torque by intentionally contact the arm to the external environment to support the gravity. In other words, there is a possibility of achievement of operations that intentionally exploit contact between the arm and the external environment. Therefore, this paper aims to demonstrate the high external environment adaptability of coupled tendon-driven arms in unknown environments. First, we will show through analysis and hardware experiments that coupled tendon-driven arms have high compliance due to the elongation of driving tendons. Additionally, by comparing analytical and measured values, we discuss the extent to which the analysis can be applied. Then, through hardware experiments, we will achieve three points: contact with the external environment without damage to the arm or obstacles, the possibility of detecting external contact, and operations that utilize external contact. 2. Super Dragon This section explains the coupled tendon drive, which is the driving method of Super Dragon, and the hardware, respectively. 2.1. Coupled tendon drive Coupled tendon drive is a driving method for long-reach manipulators proposed by Hirose et al. which drives each joint using tendons such as metal wires or ropes [Citation11]. A figure shows a coupled tendon-driven manipulator composed of three joints. The joint located closest to the base is called the 1st joint, and from there toward the tip, they are called the 2nd joint, 3rd joint, and so on. Each joint has a pulley fixed to the link (hereinafter driving pulley), to which the end of the tendon driving that joint is fixed. Tendons driving joints on the tip side are wound around joints on the base side via freely rotating pulleys (hereinafter torque transmission pulleys). When all pulley radii are equal to r and each tendon is pulled with force F, the torques generated at each joint are 1=3Fr, 2=2Fr, and 3=Fr from the base, respectively. Thus, joints closer to the base, which require larger torque to support gravity, have more tendons wound around them, enabling the generation of larger torque. Furthermore, by mounting the actuators that pull the tendons at the base rather than on the arm, the arm portion can be made lightweight. In this way, coupled tendon drive is suitable as a driving method for long-reach manipulators because it can support the arm's gravity with the tension of multiple tendons. 2.2. Hardware We explain the hardware configuration of Super Dragon. Super Dragon is a coupled tendon-driven manipulator with an arm length of 10 m, maximum arm diameter of 0.2 m, and 10 joints [Citation6-10]. The arrangement of joints, tendons, and pulleys of Super Dragon is shown. In this paper, we define yaw joints whose axes coincide with the vertical direction when the arm is extended horizontally, and pitch joints whose axes are horizontal and perpendicular to the longitudinal direction. We consider an xyz coordinate system, with the vertically upward direction as the positive z-axis direction, and the direction from the base to the tip when the arm takes a horizontal posture as the positive x-axis direction. This arm consists of pitch joints and yaw joints, enabling three-dimensional motion through three-dimensional coupled tendon drive. Tendons driving pitch joints are shown in pink, and tendons driving yaw joints are shown in cyan. Additionally, Horigome et al. proposed a gravity compensation mechanism for arms using large-diameter tendons introduced separately from the coupled tendon drive system, which Super Dragon adopts [Citation14]. This method compensates for most of the gravity by winding a large-diameter tendon shown in orange around pitch joints and pulling it with actuators with large generating force such as pneumatic cylinders, in addition to the tendons that control each joint. In this paper, we refer to the small-diameter tendons that control joints as joint control tendons and the large-diameter tendons that compensate for gravity as gravity compensation tendons. The joint control tendons use 2 mm diameter ropes (NA20020SOC00\001.000, Armare) made of high-density polyethylene with heat-set. For gravity compensation tendons, 5.5 mm diameter PBO fiber rope (ZB-15728, Hayami Industry) is used, wound from the 1st joint to the 5th joint. Each joint is equipped with a potentiometer (JC10-000-103N, Nidec Copal Electronics), allowing the arm's posture to be acquired at each control cycle. The base is equipped with gear-head electromagnetic motors (241322, Maxon Motor) that pull the joint control tendons, pneumatic cylinders (10A-2FB200B300, TAIYO) that pull the gravity compensation tendons, tension sensors, and other electrical systems. Joint angle control is performed by PI control of the winding amount of joint control tendons, and for gravity compensation tendons, PI control of the pressure value of the pneumatic cylinders that apply tension is performed. Super Dragon can generate high joint torque due to the characteristics of coupled tendon drive, which enables arm weight reduction and allows the arm's self-weight to be cooperatively supported by multiple tendons. For example, in an experiment maintaining a horizontal posture with a tip payload of 10 kg, 1st joint achieved a high joint torque of 2797 Nm. 3. Measurement of endpoint compliance This section demonstrates that Super Dragon has endpoint compliance due to the elasticity of driving tendons. First, we analytically show the compliance ellipsoid of the tip position, then measure the compliance value using the actual hardware and compare it with the analytical solution. 3.1. Derivation of endpoint compliance ellipsoid Let the tip position of Super Dragon in equilibrium state be xe R3×1, and the reaction force to its small displacement xe be fe R3×1. Then, we define the stiffness coefficient matrix Kx R3×3 of the arm tip by the following equation: fe = Kx xe (1) Next, we define each joint torque of Super Dragon as R10×1 and each rope tension as f R21×1. We also define joint control tendon tension and gravity compensation tendon tension as fj R20×1 and fwc R, respectively. We define the pulley matrix Rj R10×20 regarding the pulley diameter and winding direction of joint control tendons. The component r_{i,j} in the i-th row and j-th column of the pulley matrix represents the radius of the pulley around which rope j placed at the i-th joint is wound. The two ropes driving the n-th joint are designated as rope 2n1 and rope 2n, wound to change the driven joint in negative and positive rotation directions, respectively. The radius of pulleys wound in the negative direction is taken as a negative value. Similarly, defining the pulley matrix for gravity compensation tendons as Rwc R10×1, the joint torque can be calculated as: = R f = [Rj Rwc] [fj; fwc] (2) Since the path length of joint control tendons changes by Rj^T due to joint angle changes, the elongation of joint control tendons is calculated as r_reel Rj^T from the winding reel angle R20×1 and reel radius r_reel R. Therefore, using the stiffness matrix K R20×20 of joint control tendons, the tension fj of rope j is calculated as: fj = K ( r_reel Rj^T ) (3) From Equations (2) and (3), the generated torque when giving a small displacement to joint angle can be calculated as Equation (4): = Rj K Rj^T (4) However, assuming that the motors pulling the joint control tendons are stopped, the winding reel angle R20×1 is constant. Also, we assume that the gravity compensation tendon tension fwc does not depend on . Using the Jacobian matrix J, the relationship between xe and is expressed by the following equation: xe = J (5) From Equations (1), (4), (5) and Equation (6) derived from the principle of virtual work, Equation (7) is derived: ^T = fe^T xe (6) Kx = (J^T)^+ Rj K Rj^T J^+ (7) Where J R^{m×n} is the Jacobian matrix, and J^+, (J^T)^+ are the pseudo-inverse matrices of J and J^T, respectively. In redundant arms, generally m<n, so pseudo-inverse matrices need to be used; however, the pseudo-inverse matrices used in Equation (7) are not the most commonly used Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse matrices. Since the row vectors of J are linearly independent and it is a wide matrix, there are infinitely many solutions to Equation (5). One solution, = J^+ xe becomes the minimum norm solution when using the Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse matrix. Since each joint stiffness differs in coupled tendon-driven arms, we use the Lagrange multiplier method to find the solution to Equation (5) that minimizes the sum of elastic energy E_sum of each joint angle control tendon, thereby calculating the pseudo-inverse matrix. E_sum is as follows: Where _j is the j-th row component of , and K_i represents the stiffness of rope i. E_sum = sum_{i=1}^{n} (1/2 K_{2i-1} (sum_{j=1}^{i} r_{j,2i-1} _j)^2 + 1/2 K_{2i} (sum_{j=1}^{i} r_{j,2i} _j)^2 ) (8) In this paper, we consider the endpoint compliance ellipsoid of Super Dragon in horizontal posture ( = 0). Unlike general postures, the horizontal posture is a singular posture, so the endpoint compliance has no component in the longitudinal direction, i.e. the x-axis direction. Therefore, the endpoint compliance ellipsoid in horizontal posture becomes a two-dimensional ellipse that can be drawn in yz coordinates. Here, we ignore the influence of gravity compensation tendons because the base-side joints where gravity compensation tendons are wound have high joint stiffness due to many tendons being wound around them, resulting in relatively small joint angle changes at the base side when displacement. We assume that the elastic coefficient of joint control tendons is constant regardless of elongation, and set the elastic coefficient per unit strain as k = 3.40 × 10^4 N. The measurement and derivation of this value are described in the Appendix. Furthermore, assuming quasi-static motion, we ignore the viscosity coefficient. The ellipse axis does not coincide with the y and z axes but is tilted. The angle between the major axis of the ellipse and the y-axis is 8.6 deg. For a typical robot arm consisting of pitch and yaw joints in horizontal posture, the two axes of the endpoint compliance ellipsoid coincide with the rotation axes of pitch or yaw joints in horizontal posture. However, in coupled tendon drive, the ellipse tilts because joint angle changes affect joints with different rotation axes. 3.2. Endpoint compliance measurement experiment We conducted an endpoint compliance measurement experiment in horizontal posture to verify the validity of the previous section. The experiment was conducted by stopping the base motors with Super Dragon maintained horizontally. The experiment was conducted by connecting a force gauge (Nidec-Shimpo: FGP-50) to the Super Dragon's tip with a rope, and measuring the tension when pulling and releasing in both positive and negative directions along the y-axis and z-axis. The tip position was measured by driving the force gauge at a constant speed using a linear stage (COMS: PM40B-25X) and reading the tension at regular time intervals, measuring the tip position twice in each of the four directions along the y and z axes. Here, since rope elongation occurs due to pulling the arm tip and the passage of time, increasing the joint angle error from the horizontal posture, we corrected the joint angles to horizontal posture after two experiments in each direction. The movement speed of the linear stage was 1 mm/s for the first measurement in the y-axis positive direction and 2 mm/s for all others. The measurement results from the rise of tension to maximum tension were taken as the measurement results. Next, the measurement results were linearly approximated using the least squares method (polyfit function, Matlab). The data are displayed such that the line for pulling passes through the origin, and the line for releasing intersects at the position of maximum tension. The measurement results in the y-axis positive direction and y-axis negative direction are shown, with the first and second measurements shown from left to right for each direction. Similarly, the measurement results in the z-axis direction are shown. The average value of the measured slopes in each data set was taken as the tip stiffness. By taking the reciprocal, the compliance in the y and z-axis directions was calculated as 31.8 and 22.6 mm/N, respectively. We compare the joint angle changes when pulled 0.2 m in the first measurements in the y-axis positive direction and z-axis positive direction with the analytical results. The analytical calculation method for joint angles uses the pseudo-inverse matrix of the Jacobian matrix derived as shown in Section 3.1 for Equation (5) to calculate the joint angle changes when giving 0.2 m displacement in the y and z-axis directions, respectively. The measured values and analytical results are shown. We compare these results with the analytical results from the previous section. The intersection point on the horizontal axis (labeled y-axis compliance) of the endpoint compliance ellipsoid indicates the compliance when force is applied laterally to the arm tip. Similarly, the compliance in the z-axis direction can be obtained, with the compliance in the y-axis and z-axis directions being 315 and 234 mm/N, respectively. The blue bars represent the analytical values of endpoint compliance obtained in Section 3.1, and the red bars represent the measured values obtained from measurement experiments. In contrast, as shown in Section 3.3, the analytical values of endpoint compliance obtained using the equivalent tendon stiffness K_exp including frictional forces acting on the rope are shown in green. The measured values compared to the analytical solutions were 9.91% in the y-axis direction and 10.4% in the z-axis direction, showing lower compliance in the measured values but confirming that the order of magnitude matches. 3.3. Consideration We discuss the results from the previous section. The measured endpoint compliance values are approximately 10% of the analytically obtained values. The cause of such numerical differences is considered to be the influence of frictional forces acting on the joint control tendons. In Section 3.1, the analysis was performed under the assumption that tension is uniformly distributed along the rope with respect to elongation. However, in reality, the rope tension is not uniformly distributed due to rotational friction at pulleys where the rope is wound around and frictional forces arising from contact with the rope itself at sections wound around pulleys. The non-uniform distribution of rope tension may have resulted in the measured values showing smaller compliance. Here, we consider calculating the equivalent stiffness of the joint control tendons using joint angular displacement data and tension data obtained from logs in the endpoint compliance measurement experiment presented in Section 3.2. If higher tension than expected was acting due to frictional forces, the obtained joint control tendon stiffness represents an equivalent stiffness that includes the influence of friction. Therefore, we calculate the equivalent stiffness of each joint control tendon obtained from compliance measurements and re-analytically determine the endpoint compliance based on this to investigate the influence of frictional forces acting on the rope on endpoint compliance. Here, we calculate the equivalent stiffness of joint control tendons based on joint angle and tension data from the compliance measurement experiment in the positive z-axis direction. The reason for focusing on z-axis direction measurements is that most of Super Dragon's joints are pitch joints, and more joint control tendons are expected to influence the results compared to the y-axis direction, thus yielding more accurate results. Since all the actuators were completely stopped, elongation l occurs in each joint control tendon solely due to joint angular displacement caused by pulling the arm's endpoint. The elongation l can be expressed as l = Rj^T using joint angular displacement data and the pulley matrix Rj. Note that the elongation l here represents the elongation from the initial horizontal posture and differs from the elongation from the natural length of each joint control tendon. Linear interpolation was performed on the experimentally obtained elongation l [m] and tension f [N] data (polyfit function, Matlab) to obtain an equivalent stiffness matrix including the effects of friction, denoted as K_exp [N/m]. Here, multiplying the diagonal components of K_exp by the natural length of each rope yields the strain stiffness k_exp. However, it is difficult to measure all unloaded tendon lengths in advance for Super Dragon, where numerous tendons are wound around pulleys. Additionally, when the arm is maintained in a horizontal posture, joint control tendons experience tensions up to approximately 1000 N, but the resulting elongation from the unloaded state is considered sufficiently small compared to rope lengths on the order of several meters. Therefore, in this paper, we multiply the equivalent stiffness K_exp of the joint control tendons by the rope length of the joint control tendons in the horizontal posture, and treat the obtained approximate values as the strain stiffness k_exp. Each component of the obtained k_exp is shown. Rope numbers follow the convention where the two ropes driving the n-th joint are designated as ropes 2n1 and 2n. Graphs for pitch joint control tendons are shown in magenta, and those for yaw joint control tendons in cyan. The strain stiffness value k = 3.40 × 10^4 N used in the analysis in Section 3.1 is indicated by a black dashed line. As can be seen, joint control tendons closer to the base show values closer to k = 3.40 × 10^4 N, while those on the tip side, which are wound around more pulleys, tend to show larger values. Thus, it was newly found that frictional forces acting on the rope increase measured tension values and contribute to increased equivalent stiffness. Using the obtained K_exp, we calculate the endpoint compliance using Equation (7), similar to Section 3.1. Additionally, let K_sim denote the tendon stiffness matrix calculated from the strain stiffness k = 3.40 × 10^4 N obtained from viscoelastic measurements used in Section 3.1. The resulting ellipsoids are shown. The ellipsoid obtained using K_sim is shown as a blue solid line, and that obtained using K_exp as a green solid line. The measured endpoint compliance values obtained in Section 3.2 are plotted as red points. Using K_exp yields a smaller compliance ellipsoid. The obtained endpoint compliance values in the y-axis and z-axis directions are shown in green. While the analytical values using K_sim differ from the measured values by approximately an order of magnitude, the analytical values based on K_exp remain within approximately three times the measured values. Regarding this approximately threefold difference, we believe it is due to factors that do not affect joint control tendon tension values, such as link deflection, minor play in mechanism assembly, and friction in bearings of joint mechanism components connecting links other than pulleys, as well as nonlinearity in tendon stiffness with respect to elongation. From the above, it was found that using equivalent tendon stiffness including the effects of frictional forces acting on the rope in endpoint compliance analysis enables obtaining analytical values closer to measured values. From Table 1, the joint angle changes in the experiment where the arm tip was pulled in the y-axis positive direction show a maximum error of 0.3 deg compared to the analytical results, indicating good agreement. For the z-axis positive direction, the maximum error between measured values and analytical results was 1.0 deg. This is considered to be due to the influence of gravity, and although the error is larger compared to the y-axis direction, it mostly agrees with the analytical results. From the above, the analytical results are considered to be valid. Thus, we have shown through analysis and experiments that the coupled tendon-driven articulated arm Super Dragon can achieve compliance without special control due to the elongation of joint control tendons. Simultaneously, we revealed that analytically calculated endpoint compliance, when the rope becomes long, exhibits errors despite matching the order of magnitude with measured values due to the influence of frictional forces acting on the rope. 4. Contact experiments with external environments Utilizing the compliance shown in the previous section, we conducted motion experiments involving contact with obstacles. We conducted two experiments: one where the arm tip contacts an obstacle, and another where part of the arm's gravity is supported by the external environment. Additionally, assuming that investigation equipment is mounted, a 5 kg weight is attached to the tip of Super Dragon. 4.1. Contact experiment with horizontally positioned obstacles We placed an obstacle on the right side as viewed from the base of the arm, and conducted a motion experiment where the arm tip contacts the obstacle and then separates. Here, the installed obstacle is a wooden rectangular parallelepiped, which has a structure sufficiently rigid relative to the force generated by the Super Dragon's endpoint. The data of joint angle measurements and joint torques calculated from tension measurements using Equation (2) during the experiment are shown. Note that the data for each joint angle are plotted with solid lines, while the target value for 6th joint is plotted with a dashed line. The arm tip contacted the obstacle at 134 s and separated from the obstacle at 153 s. During this motion, no hardware damage was observed to either the arm or the obstacle. The absolute values of joint torques for the yaw joints (6th, 8th, and 10th joints) increased during contact compared to before contact, due to the arm tip receiving external force in the y-axis direction. It is considered that contact between the arm and the external environment can be detected by the operator measuring these changes in joint torque. In this way, we achieved contact with the external environment without damage by utilizing the compliance of the coupled tendon-driven articulated arm, and demonstrated the possibility of detecting such contact. 4.2. Joint torque reduction through contact with the external environment In this section, we attempted to reduce joint torque through contact between the arm and the external environment. In other words, rather than detecting and avoiding contact with the external environment, we actively exploit such contact. The required joint torque is determined by the arm's posture and tip payload, which in turn determines the target values for driving tendon tension. However, in practice, there are limits to the tendon strength, which constrains the joint torque that can be generated at each joint. Therefore, postures requiring high joint torque are difficult to maintain, and the workspace is limited. Here, the workspace is defined as the set of positions reachable by the end effector in three-dimensional space. Therefore, by utilizing contact with the external environment to reduce the joint torque required for posture maintenance, tendon tension can also be reduced, potentially resulting in expanding workspace. We conducted an experiment where the 9th joint (pitch) was brought into contact with an obstacle placed on the ground from above, and then the 10th joint (yaw) was driven to swing the tip left and right. Note that in this experiment, since it is sufficient to obtain a reaction force from the floor, we determined that the obstacle does not need to be rigid and used a cushioned air bed. The joint angle progression and joint torques during this time are shown. The joint torque calculation method is the same as in the previous section. Note that the data for each joint angle are plotted with solid lines, while the target value for 9th joint and 10th joint is plotted with a dashed line. The horizontal axis shows time, and the 9th joint and obstacle were in contact from 379 to 529 s. As shown in the lower part, the absolute values of joint torques were reduced during this interval. This is because the 9th joint receives an upward reaction force from contacting the obstacle, supporting part of the gravity. Additionally, we successfully drove the 10th joint by 10 deg while in contact with the external environment from 403 to 496 s. Thus, we found that joints can be driven while reducing joint torque by utilizing external contact. This makes it possible to significantly expand the arm's workspace. 5. Conclusion In this paper, we analytically demonstrated that the coupled tendon-driven articulated arm Super Dragon has compliance due to the elasticity of joint control tendons, and confirmed this using actual hardware. Furthermore, utilizing this compliance, we showed that the arm can safely contact the external environment, and demonstrated the possibility of detecting external contact through changes in torque values calculated from tension measurements. Moreover, we experimentally demonstrated that joint torque can be reduced by intentionally contacting the external environment rather than detecting and avoiding such contact. In this experiment, we successfully reduced the required joint torque magnitude of each joint by contacting the external environment and successfully operated the tip joint in that state. As future work, we plan to derive a calculation method to identify the contact point position with the arm from changes in torque values during external contact, and conduct principle verification using actual hardware.

    "license": "http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/","articleSection": "Research Article","name": "A Pipeline for Automating Emergency Medicine Documentation Using LLMs with Retrieval-Augmented Text Generation","headline": "A Pipeline for Automating Emergency Medicine Documentation Using LLMs with Retrieval-Augmented Text Generation","abstract": "Accurate and efficient documentation of patient information is vital in emergency healthcare settings. Traditional manual documentation methods are often time-consuming and prone to errors, potentially affecting patient outcomes. Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a promising solution to enhance medical communication systems; however, their clinical deployment, particularly in non-English languages such as German, presents challenges related to content accuracy, clinical relevance, and data privacy. This study addresses these challenges by developing and evaluating an automated pipeline for emergency medical documentation in German. The research objectives include (1) generating synthetic dialogues with known ground truth data to create controlled datasets for evaluating NLP performance and (2) designing an innovative pipeline to retrieve essential clinical information from these dialogues. A subset of 100 anonymized patient records from the MIMIC-IV-ED dataset was selected, ensuring diversity in demographics, chief complaints, and conditions. A Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system extracted key nominal and numerical features using chunking, embedding, and dynamic prompts. Evaluation metrics included precision, recall, F1-score, and sentiment analysis. Initial results demonstrated high extraction accuracy, particularly in medication data (F1-scores: 86.21%\u2013100%), though performance declined in nuanced clinical language, requiring further refinement for real-world emergency settings.",
    "description": "Accurate and efficient documentation of patient information is vital in emergency healthcare settings. Traditional manual documentation methods are often time-consuming and prone to errors, potentially affecting patient outcomes. Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a promising solution to enhance medical communication systems; however, their clinical deployment, particularly in non-English languages such as German, presents challenges related to content accuracy, clinical relevance, and data privacy. This study addresses these challenges by developing and evaluating an automated pipeline for emergency medical documentation in German. The research objectives include (1) generating synthetic dialogues with known ground truth data to create controlled datasets for evaluating NLP performance and (2) designing an innovative pipeline to retrieve essential clinical information from these dialogues. A subset of 100 anonymized patient records from the MIMIC-IV-ED dataset was selected, ensuring diversity in demographics, chief complaints, and conditions. A Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system extracted key nominal and numerical features using chunking, embedding, and dynamic prompts. Evaluation metrics included precision, recall, F1-score, and sentiment analysis. Initial results demonstrated high extraction accuracy, particularly in medication data (F1-scores: 86.21%\u2013100%), though performance declined in nuanced clinical language, requiring further refinement for real-world emergency settings.",

                            <div id="mathJaxToggle"><label for="mathJaxToggle"><span>Formulae display:</span><input type="checkbox" id="mathJaxToggleCheck" name="mathJaxToggleCheck"></label><span class="mathJaxLogo"><img src="/templates/jsp/_style2/_tandf/pb2/images/math-jax.gif" loading="lazy" alt="MathJax Logo"><span data-tooltip-wrapper=""><span class="qMrk" data-behaviour="tooltip">?</span><span class="auPopUp" data-tooltip=""><span class="pointyEdge"></span>Mathematical formulae have been encoded as MathML and are displayed in this HTML version using MathJax in order to improve their display. Uncheck the box to turn MathJax off. This feature requires Javascript. Click on a formula to zoom.</span></span></span></div><div class="hlFld-Fulltext"><div id="abstractId1" class="hlFld-Abstract"><h2 class="section-heading-2" id="abstract">ABSTRACT</h2><p class="last">Accurate and efficient documentation of patient information is vital in emergency healthcare settings. Traditional manual documentation methods are often time-consuming and prone to errors, potentially affecting patient outcomes. Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a promising solution to enhance medical communication systems; however, their clinical deployment, particularly in non-English languages such as German, presents challenges related to content accuracy, clinical relevance, and data privacy. This study addresses these challenges by developing and evaluating an automated pipeline for emergency medical documentation in German. The research objectives include (1) generating synthetic dialogues with known ground truth data to create controlled datasets for evaluating NLP performance and (2) designing an innovative pipeline to retrieve essential clinical information from these dialogues. A subset of 100 anonymized patient records from the MIMIC-IV-ED dataset was selected, ensuring diversity in demographics, chief complaints, and conditions. A Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system extracted key nominal and numerical features using chunking, embedding, and dynamic prompts. Evaluation metrics included precision, recall, F1-score, and sentiment analysis. Initial results demonstrated high extraction accuracy, particularly in medication data (F1-scores: 86.21%–100%), though performance declined in nuanced clinical language, requiring further refinement for real-world emergency settings.</p></div><div id="embedded-pdf-target" class="with-border"></div><div class="pb-dropzone" data-pb-dropzone="contentNavigationDropZoneAbs"><div class="widget gql-content-navigation none  widget-none" id="2ffb43c7-15dc-4c79-9e29-8e4bed9b3d1e">
        
        

        <div class="wrapped ">
            
            <div class="widget-body body body-none "><div class="ajaxWidget" data-ajax-widget="gql-content-navigation" data-ajax-widget-id="2ffb43c7-15dc-4c79-9e29-8e4bed9b3d1e" data-ajax-observe="true">
    
</div></div>
        </div>
    </div></div><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_1" id="s0001"><h2 class="section-heading-2" id="d1e236">Introduction</h2><p>In healthcare settings, especially in high-pressure environments like emergency care and rescue services, the ability to document and relay patient information swiftly and accurately is critical. Comprehensive and precise documentation plays a key role in clinical decision-making, ensuring continuity of care, and ultimately improving patient outcomes. However, traditional manual documentation methods often prove to be time-consuming and prone to inaccuracies, particularly when relying on retrospective processes. These inefficiencies can significantly impact the quality of patient care (Endsley <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0007" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2011</a></span>). The integration of advanced technologies, such as large language models (LLMs) and hands-free platforms, into medical communication processes holds promise in addressing these challenges. By enhancing real-time documentation, these technologies support clinicians in maintaining accurate records without the usual time delays, improving both the efficiency and reliability of patient information exchange.</p><p>The state of the art in hands-free electronic documentation for emergency care is evolving rapidly, with several promising technologies emerging to address the critical need for efficient and accurate documentation (Falcetta et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0008" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2023</a></span>). Automated systems, such as the Automated Sensing Clinical Documentation system (Bloos et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0003" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2020</a></span>) and platforms leveraging hands-free technology (like smart glasses), have shown feasibility in real-world settings, although they face challenges related to noise resilience and usability in chaotic medical environments (Zhang et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0043" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2022</a></span>). Most of the systems rely on advanced automatic speech recognition and natural language processing (NLP) to capture medical information in real-time, reducing the burden on clinicians and minimizing errors (Woo et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0037" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2021</a></span>). While digital scribes demonstrate increased efficiency and ease of use, especially in patient-centered communication, many of these systems still require further optimization and large-scale validation to become fully integrated into clinical workflows (Wang et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0034" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2021</a></span>). Nonetheless, these innovations signal a shift toward more seamless, real-time documentation processes that support emergency care providers under intense conditions.</p><p>Nowadays, cloud – based large language models achieve state-of-the-art performance on medical information-extraction tasks (García-Barragán et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0010" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2024</a></span>; Yang et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0040" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2024</a></span>). However, transmitting protected health information to third-party servers poses serious privacy risks in real-world medical applications. In contrast, deploying LLMs on-premises preserves patient confidentiality. Although local LLMs – especially smaller, off-the-shelf open-source variants – often underperform relative to their cloud-hosted counterparts, they can still deliver acceptable results when properly managed. Two strategies that help close this performance gap are fine-tuning on domain-specific examples and in-context learning via prompt engineering. In particular, carefully designed prompts can reduce hallucinations and improve robustness, even for out-of-the-box local models (Alkhalaf et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0001" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2024</a></span>).</p><p>A further challenge for on-premises deployments is the limited context window of local LLMs, which caps how much text can be processed in a single inference pass. Complete Emergency Medical Services (EMS) dialogue transcripts – whose lengths and participant counts vary widely – might exceed this limit, forcing either the deployment of larger models or iterative processing of the text, both of which drive up latency and compute requirements. To mitigate this, we propose that a lightweight semantic-retrieval step can pre-filter transcripts for the most clinically relevant passages before costly LLM inference. In other words, the model receives a curated subset of candidate passages – omitting irrelevant content – thereby, in theory, preserving the extraction performance of local LLMs while keeping hardware demands stable regardless of transcript length.</p><p>Information-extraction (IE) pipelines require representative data for both model development and evaluation, yet no publicly available corpus of German EMS dialogue transcripts exists. The only related resource, RescueSpeech, comprises 2 h of firefighter-simulation audio, is domain-mismatched, and still requires speech-to-text conversion (Sagar et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0030" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2023</a></span>). Recording and manually annotating authentic EMS dialogues entails complex privacy-compliance procedures and substantial annotation effort – burdens that become prohibitive when project resources are limited. As an alternative, synthetic dialogue generation via LLMs offers a scalable, privacy-compliant path.</p><p>Prior work on medical dialogue generation stem mainly from China. Platforms such as Chunyu Doctor provide millions of unlabeled dialogues that underpin MedDialog (Zeng et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0042" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2020</a></span>), KaMed (Li et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0018" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2021</a></span>), ReMeDi-large (Yan et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0039" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2022</a></span>), and MedDG (Liu et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0020" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2022</a></span>). Building on these datasets, systems like NoteChat generate dialogues from medical notes (Wang et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0035" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2024</a></span>), while MedKP enriches ChatGLM3-6B with external knowledge to boost performance on KaMed and MedDG (Wu et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0038" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2024</a></span>); reasoning-focused methods such as BP4ER further refine generation quality (He et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0012" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2024</a></span>). In the English-speaking world, resources are scarcer: AskADoctor data and 10 000 iCliniq dialogues enabled ChatDoctor (Li et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0019" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2023</a></span>). Together, these examples highlight the central role of domain-specific dialogue corpora and underscore the current gap for medical language processing – prompting us to generate synthetic German medical emergency service dialogues primarily as a testbed for developing and evaluating our LLM-based information-extraction pipeline.</p><p>Generating useful medical dialogues requires the integration of clinically coherent information and the availability of ground-truth annotations to support downstream information extraction benchmarks. A practical method to fulfill both requirements involves incorporating structured clinical data from available real datasets into the prompt context for language model-based generation. This approach ensures semantic and factual consistency within the dialogue and simultaneously provides gold-standard labels that can be leveraged for evaluation tasks. However, data use agreements often strictly prohibit transmitting raw or potentially identifiable patient information to external cloud-based services, thereby restricting access to high-capacity, proprietary language models when local or open-source LLMs prove insufficient. This regulatory constraint necessitates a preprocessing step wherein records are transformed into fully synthetic, de-identified datasets that preserve clinical plausibility while ensuring privacy compliance prior to their use in data-driven dialogue generation</p><p class="last">To address privacy constraints, data scarcity, and context window limitations in LLMs, we propose a three-part framework tailored to the complexities of German emergency service dialogues. (1) A retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline extracts clinically relevant information from noisy, unstructured transcripts, where medical details are often scattered and interwoven with non-clinical content; we hypothesize that sufficiently large pretrained models can perform this task without fine-tuning, leveraging their inherent medical knowledge. (2) A synthetic dialogue generator produces annotated German emergency conversations from structured MIMIC-IV data, embedding realistic medical content alongside distractions such as irrelevant details, redundancies, and terminological variation to simulate real-world conditions. (3) An adapted Post-Randomization Method (PRAM) converts MIMIC-IV records into fully synthetic, de-identified inputs that comply with licensing restrictions while preserving clinical realism. Unlike prior work – e.g., Heilmeyer et al., who lacked controlled ground truth (Heilmeyer et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0013" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2024</a></span>), or Wang et al., who focused on static texts (Wang et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0036" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2024</a></span>) – our pipeline enables scalable, automated evaluation of information extraction in dynamic, multilingual emergency scenarios, providing a privacy-preserving testbed for LLM-based clinical documentation.</p></div><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_1" id="s0002"><h2 class="section-heading-2" id="d1e313">Methods</h2><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_2" id="s0002-s2001"><h3 class="section-heading-3" id="d1e315">Data basis: MIMIC-IV dataset</h3><p>MIMIC-IV, hosted on PhysioNet and provided by the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, is a publicly available database containing de-identified patient data from Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center in Boston. It is intended for research in healthcare and biomedical fields, offering a wide range of clinical information (Johnson et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0015" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2023</a></span>). A specific subset, MIMIC-IV-ED, focuses on the Emergency Department (ED), providing detailed records of patient encounters, including diagnoses, vital signs, and treatments. This dataset was utilized in our study to generate an annotated dialogue text dataset for system design and evaluation.</p><p>We utilize structured data from randomly sampled 100 patients as the baseline for our system, serving two principal objectives. First, this dataset facilitates the generation of dialogues for emergency scenarios based on consistent patient characteristics. Second, it provides a gold standard for evaluating the efficacy of our NLP pipeline in text extraction. To address privacy concerns and comply with data usage terms, we additionally anonymize the baseline data before processing it, thereby eliminating any direct association with the original sources. Consequently, our analysis is based solely on this synthetic data (see next subsection).</p><p><button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="t0001" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="table">Table 1</button> delineates the features employed in this study to ensure a representative spectrum of patient profiles. These features were identified following the storage of the MIMIC-IV data in our MongoDB database. Initially, we determined the relevant features by evaluating the data’s alignment with our research objectives. The selection of specific tables from the MIMIC-IV database – namely edstays, diagnosis, medrecon, pyxis, triage, and vitalsign – was guided by their capacity to capture comprehensive patient profiles in accordance with the German DIVI and MIND emergency protocols (Messelken et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0023" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2011</a></span>). This targeted selection ensures that the dataset encompasses the critical variables necessary for robust and representative patient profiling.</p><div class="tableView"><div class="tableCaption"><div class="short-legend"><h3><p class="captionText"><span class="captionLabel">Table 1. </span> Overview of the MIMIC-IV data.</p></h3></div></div><div class="tableDownloadOption" id="t0001-table-wrapper"><a class="downloadButton btn btn-sm" role="button" href="/action/downloadTable?id=t0001&doi=10.1080%2F08839514.2025.2519169&downloadType=CSV">Download CSV</a><a data-id="t0001" class="downloadButton btn btn-sm displaySizeTable" href="#" role="button" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="table">Display Table</a></div></div><p></p><p>The edstays table offers fundamental details about patient admissions, including the mode of arrival and transport. Complementing this, the diagnosis table records patient diagnoses coded with ICD titles. The medrecon table includes data on medications reported by patients prior to their ED visit, which is crucial for comprehending the patient’s medication history and ensuring continuity of care. In contrast, the pyxis table details the medications administered during the visit. By comparing this data with the pre-visit medications recorded in medrecon, discrepancies can be identified, ensuring that the medication administration is accurately reflected and aligns with actual practices. Furthermore, the triage table documents patient acuity levels and initial vital signs recorded upon arrival. This information is essential for assessing the severity of the patient’s condition and prioritizing treatment based on urgency. In addition, the vitals table provides detailed measurements of vital signs throughout the ED visit. Key parameters, such as blood pressure, temperature, heart rate, respiratory rate, and oxygen saturation (O2 saturation), are critical for monitoring the patient’s health status and evaluating their response to treatment.</p><p>Although not all protocol fields could be mapped to the ED data, the identified features provided a sufficient foundation for generating dialogues and exploring the quality of our information extraction NLP pipeline in emergency service scenarios.</p><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_3" id="s0002-s2001-s3001"><h4 class="section-heading-4" id="d1e511">Ethical approval statement</h4><p class="last">This study did not involve human participants, human data, or human tissue. Therefore, ethical approval was not required. No approval from an ethics committee was necessary. Consequently, the study does not fall under the scope of the Declaration of Helsinki.</p></div></div><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_2" id="s0002-s2002"><h3 class="section-heading-3" id="d1e514">Synthetic data generation using PRAM</h3><p>The MIMIC-IV dataset we obtained is anonymized to protect patient identities, but it remains subject to strict data-sharing restrictions that prohibit dissemination to third parties, such as OpenAI. To comply with these restrictions, we swapped feature values according to the PRAM method (Domingo-Ferrer and Soria-Comas <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0006" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2018</a></span>). Compared to data swapping, PRAM offers a significant advantage: it is grounded in statistical principles rather than being purely mechanical. This allows for the application of comprehensive statistical modeling and inference techniques. In this context, our primary focus is on achieving non-mechanical perturbation of the features, ultimately rendering it entirely synthetic.</p><p>The PRAM implementation used in our study is based on the sdcMicro package from R. We determine the transition matrix probabilities <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/32612ca8-ea18-4e85-9d4e-958b8d731781/uaai_a_2519169_ilm0001.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi mathvariant="italic">P</mi></math></span> such that there is a positive probability of a swap between the <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/3d5395ea-e5ec-4177-b020-bb6167bcfdda/uaai_a_2519169_ilm0002.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi mathvariant="italic">i</mi></math></span>-th and <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/46958e1b-9e2e-4290-a59e-7e1d3e208f6e/uaai_a_2519169_ilm0003.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi mathvariant="italic">j</mi></math></span>-th category values (<span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/86609599-cc7d-432e-8092-5724e33b8901/uaai_a_2519169_ilm0004.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mrow><msub><mi>p</mi><mrow><mi mathvariant="italic">ij</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="italic">i</mi><mo>≠</mo><mi mathvariant="italic">j</mi></math></span>), while ensuring that <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/8631313a-a472-4e09-b2db-47b539d1ab8b/uaai_a_2519169_ilm0005.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mrow><msub><mi>p</mi><mrow><mi mathvariant="italic">ii</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math></span>. This approach is taken to generate fully synthetic data. Gender is preserved in the dataset to avoid inconsistencies, such as mismatches between gender-specific diagnoses or medications, particularly in the context of dialogue generation.</p><p>To preserve data coherence and thereby data utility as much as possible, original values are swapped only with similar values. For numerical data, like blood pressure readings, we can directly swap values using the PRAM function provided by the sdcMicro package. This method automatically ensures that the swapped values remain within a plausible range for the attribute. Nominal data (like diagnoses or drug names), which are represented as text strings, presented a challenge. Automated swapping of these values using PRAM was not directly feasible because it requires a transition matrix that connects similar words or categories, which is difficult to generate automatically for text data. To construct the transition matrices in this case, we randomly sampled 1,000 unique patient cases, enabling the exchange of semantically similar values.</p><p>To illustrate the principle of utility-based PRAM using text data, we focus here on the diagnostic descriptions. We include only unique diagnoses of the previously sampled 1000 patient cases into a sub-sample dedicated for clustering, to prevent data skewing that could result from multiple identical entries. The distinct diagnoses are then categorized into semantically similar groups based on the similarities of their corresponding word embeddings (see below). For this purpose, we employ the “bionlp/bluebert_pubmed_mimic_uncased_L-12_H-768_A-12” model (Peng, Yan, and Lu <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0027" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2019</a></span>), which is specifically trained on biomedical texts, allowing for a more accurate representation of word meanings within this context. Initially, the word embeddings are 768-dimensional. To enhance data manageability, the dimensionality of these embeddings is reduced using Principal Component Analysis. For instance, in the context of diagnoses, the dimensions are reduced to 65. This reduction simplifies the data while retaining as much meaningful information as possible.</p><p>The similarities of the dimensionally reduced word embeddings are calculated as follows: First, the word embeddings are L2-normalized, ensuring that each embedding vector has a unit length. This normalization step transforms the L2 distance into a linearly affine version of the cosine distance, which is more appropriate for semantic comparisons. Following normalization, the embeddings are grouped using constrained k-means clustering, implemented via the “k-means-constrained” library (Su, Kogan, and Nicholas <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0032" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2010</a></span>). Unlike standard k-means clustering, which can produce clusters of varying sizes, this method allows for the specification of cluster sizes. In this case, the clustering is constrained to produce 100 clusters of equal size. Each of the diagnostic string is then assigned a cluster number based on its grouping. Within each cluster, outliers are identified by calculating the cosine distance of each string from the cluster center. Strings exceeding three standard deviations above the mean cosine distance within their cluster (>3σ) are flagged as outliers. To maintain cluster integrity, the single most extreme outlier in each cluster is removed, not more, ensuring the clusters remain sufficiently large.</p><p>The clustering results are subsequently mapped back to the original 1000 samples, with each sample assigned a corresponding cluster number based on the proximity to the nearest cluster center. During PRAM for each sample, the cluster number is picked and used to build a transition matrix with all string values within given cluster, including the original value. For each transition matrix, a uniform distribution was applied to all non-original values, while the original value is assigned a probability of 0, enforcing replacement of the old with the other values in given cluster (see <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="f0001" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">Figure 1</button>).
</p><div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure 1. </span></span> A conceptual depiction of a transition matrix for diagnoses falling into cluster nr. 23. Diagonal cells are zero (no self-transition), while off-diagonal cells have equal probabilities (in this case 1/4 or 25%) for transitioning to other complaints during the PRAM process.</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="f0001"><img id="d1e603" src="/cms/asset/5ec4dda2-8b00-4c39-87ce-ce31fccacfc9/uaai_a_2519169_f0001_oc.jpg" loading="lazy" height="398" width="500" alt="Figure 1. A conceptual depiction of a transition matrix for diagnoses falling into cluster nr. 23. Diagonal cells are zero (no self-transition), while off-diagonal cells have equal probabilities (in this case 1/4 or 25%) for transitioning to other complaints during the PRAM process."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="f0001" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div><p></p><p class="last">The resulting modified values were then stored in the MongoDB database as the final synthetic dataset, for the generation of 100 dialogues.</p></div><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_2" id="s0002-s2003"><h3 class="section-heading-3" id="d1e607">Dialogue generation pipeline</h3><p>Based on the synthetic MIMIC dataset, we generate 100 dialogue texts within an emergency context, aiming to simulate verbal interactions between healthcare professionals and patients. To achieve this, we develop a two-stage pipeline for automated dialogue generation: an initial local generation of English dialogues followed by refinement and translation into German via OpenAI’s API services. In the first stage, we employ the “Zephyr-7b-beta” model from the Hugging Face transformers library, which offers performance comparable to larger models like Llama-2-70B-Chat. The model is configured with a maximum token length of 1500 and a temperature setting of 0.7 to introduce variability in the dialogues (Peeperkorn et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0026" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2024</a></span>). In the second stage, we refine and translate the initially generated English dialogues into German using OpenAI’s GPT-4 Turbo model with again a temperature setting of 0.7. The primary objectives here are to enhance naturalness, fluency, and extend the dialogue length in token size, while keeping relevant clinical information intact for later evaluation.</p><p>There are two primary reasons for initiating the first stage: (i) to further distance the process from the synthetic MIMIC-IV dataset before transitioning to cloud-based models accessed via the OpenAI API services, and (ii) to generate dialogues that minimize challenges in text extraction. The initial dialogue generation process is divided into four sections to ensure a coherent narrative structure: (1) initial ambulance interaction, (2) triage and initial vital sign measurements, (3) administration of medication and subsequent vital sign measurements, and (4) hospital arrival. For each phase, we design a prompt template that instructs the language model on the specific events, the actions of healthcare professionals (such as medical procedures), and the synthetic data features to be integrated (<button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="f0002" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">Figure 2</button> shows a template for the phase (3)).
</p><div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure 2. </span></span> One of the prompt templates used for dialogue generation containing placeholders to be injected with MIMIC-IV based data.</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="f0002"><img id="d1e625" src="/cms/asset/343f1088-d2c2-4710-9fee-98ddcb8734b1/uaai_a_2519169_f0002_b.gif" loading="lazy" height="163" width="500" alt="Figure 2. One of the prompt templates used for dialogue generation containing placeholders to be injected with MIMIC-IV based data."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="f0002" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div><p></p><p>This initial process can be summarized and conceptually illustrated by the following pseudocode, which captures the underlying implementation concept (<button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="f0003" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">Figure 3</button>).
</p><div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure 3. </span></span> Conceptual pseudocode illustrating the iterative process of initial dialogue generation in English, utilizing the local Zephyr-7b-beta model.</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="f0003"><img id="d1e639" src="/cms/asset/785dc69f-5897-4815-bfaf-ffdb2b7ba2ac/uaai_a_2519169_f0003_b.gif" loading="lazy" height="263" width="500" alt="Figure 3. Conceptual pseudocode illustrating the iterative process of initial dialogue generation in English, utilizing the local Zephyr-7b-beta model."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="f0003" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div><p></p><p>For generating the extended German dialogues, we use the initial dialogues as a foundational basis. Although models like GPT-4-turbo from OpenAI have large context windows (e.g., accepting up to 128,000 tokens), they are still limited by a maximum output length. To circumvent the constraints of context length, we employ a hierarchical expansion method. Unlike overlapping chunking, this approach significantly reduces the occurrence of contextual drift and incoherent output dialogues (Nuttal <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0025" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2023</a></span>). The hierarchical strategy facilitates detailed and coherent dialogue generation, ensuring that each segment is meticulously refined and seamlessly integrated into the overarching narrative. This process is illustrated in <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="f0004" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">Figure 4</button>. For each generated initial dialogue, the system produces summaries of individual sections. These summaries serve as guides for the subsequent refinement process. Each section’s outline is used to generate a refined dialogue part, which is then summarized and prefixed to the prompt template for the next section, ensuring continuity throughout the dialogue. The refined segments are then assembled to create the final dialogue.
</p><div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure 4. </span></span> Hierarchical expansion process for dialogue generation. This flowchart illustrates the multi-step process of generating and refining dialogues using a combination of local and cloud-based LLMs. Starting with synthetic data collection, an initial dialogue is generated and then iteratively refined through outlining, summarization, and continuity checks. The process culminates in the creation of a high-quality final dialogue, leveraging advanced LLMs for optimal results.</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="f0004"><img id="d1e656" src="/cms/asset/26dd84cc-4680-46f4-aaad-6b9d6cd0cfd9/uaai_a_2519169_f0004_oc.jpg" loading="lazy" height="243" width="500" alt="Figure 4. Hierarchical expansion process for dialogue generation. This flowchart illustrates the multi-step process of generating and refining dialogues using a combination of local and cloud-based LLMs. Starting with synthetic data collection, an initial dialogue is generated and then iteratively refined through outlining, summarization, and continuity checks. The process culminates in the creation of a high-quality final dialogue, leveraging advanced LLMs for optimal results."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="f0004" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div><p></p><p>The templates for generating refined dialogues build significantly on those used for the initial dialogues, with three key enhancements aimed at producing more natural and realistic exchanges. First, each refined dialogue begins with a section summary that encapsulates the content of the initial dialogues. Second, from section two onward, cumulative summaries of the preceding refined sections are included, enriching the context. Placeholders are defined for both summary types. Third, the prompt includes detailed delivery instructions – such as tone, language requirements (German), and context sensitivity. A crucial feature is the “Current Events” block, which summarizes all preceding medical facts, allowing the model to adjust tone and urgency – from life-threatening events like cardiac arrest to minor issues like dizziness – via the clause: “Ensure that the dialogues are authentic, appropriate for an emergency medical response in German, and grounded in the Current Events.” The prompt also defines clear register boundaries: “Medical professionals should communicate with each other in specialist terms while speaking to the patient in plain, courteous language.” Additionally, it conditions the patient’s responses on clinical status, e.g., “Adjust the patient’s responses to their medical condition – for example, if confused or critically ill, they should speak accordingly.” These layered instructions ensure that the generated dialogues are situationally appropriate and linguistically authentic. The final dialogue sections are compiled and stored in the MongoDB database.</p><p class="last">The iterative process for refining and expanding the dialogues is conceptually represented in <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="f0005" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">Figure 5</button> as pseudocode, illustrating the key steps from generating section outlines to storing the complete refined dialogues.
</p><div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure 5. </span></span> Pseudocode representation of the iterative refinement, translation into German, and expansion process for dialogue generation, utilizing the OpenAI GPT-4 Turbo API.</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="f0005"><img id="d1e671" src="/cms/asset/cca35d7b-6ddd-4881-8fdf-1667cd5ac261/uaai_a_2519169_f0005_b.gif" loading="lazy" height="303" width="500" alt="Figure 5. Pseudocode representation of the iterative refinement, translation into German, and expansion process for dialogue generation, utilizing the OpenAI GPT-4 Turbo API."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="f0005" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div><p></p></div><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_2" id="s0002-s2004"><h3 class="section-heading-3" id="d1e674">RAG for text extraction</h3><p>To extract the desired information in accordance with emergency medical protocols, several methods can be employed. Traditional techniques, such as named entity recognition (NER), often encounter challenges in capturing the necessary contextual nuances, resulting in incomplete or inaccurate outcomes (Cho and Lee <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0004" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2019</a></span>). This is particularly problematic in cases where the information to be extracted is highly heterogeneous, as these methods tend to miss subtle but critical details that are context-dependent (Landolsi, Hlaoua, and Romdhane <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0017" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2024</a></span>). In contrast, RAG offers a more promising approach and combines two key steps to improve how information is processed and generated. First, it retrieves relevant data or documents from a database or vector store, which helps it understand the context better. Then, it uses this information to generate more accurate and relevant responses or outputs. Essentially, RAG finds the right information first and then uses it to create a better answer, making it particularly useful for tasks where context and detail are important.</p><p>When deploying local LLMs, particularly in scenarios where the sensitivity of patient data precludes the use of cloud-based models, it is crucial to implement efficient text extraction techniques that prioritize the most relevant sections of the text. Due to the context length limitations inherent in LLMs, processing entire documents simultaneously is often impractical. Therefore, a sophisticated chunking strategy must be developed in conjunction with RAG methods to optimize processing efficiency and ensure accurate outcomes. Our RAG approach involves four key steps:
</p><ol class="NLM_list NLM_list-list_type-order"><li><p class="first last"><b>Chunking</b>: We divide each dialogue text into overlapping chunks of 200 characters, which are then embedded and stored in a vector database.</p></li><li><p class="first last"><b>Prompt Template Creation</b>: We design a dynamic prompt template with placeholders (see <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="f0006" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">Figure 6</button>) that can be adapted to various extraction targets. This template allows for the specification of granular queries for retrieval from the indexed chunks in the vector store.</p><div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure 6. </span></span> Empty RAG prompt template for answer generation with placeholders.</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="f0006"><img id="d1e704" src="/cms/asset/d164c508-48f6-4ea0-ad99-8f8c43d78285/uaai_a_2519169_f0006_oc.jpg" loading="lazy" height="165" width="500" alt="Figure 6. Empty RAG prompt template for answer generation with placeholders."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="f0006" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div><p></p></li><li><p class="first last"><b>Chunk Retrieval</b>: Relevant chunks are retrieved from the vector store based on the top k similarity scores with the query data q_data (see <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="f0007" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">Figure 7</button> for a filled-in prompt). To optimize retrieval accuracy, only q_data is utilized, minimizing the likelihood of retrieving less relevant chunks.</p><div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure 7. </span></span> Filled RAG prompt template delivered to LLM, with abbreviated context (chunks retrieved from dialogue). In this case we want to extract “Phillips Milk of Magnesia”.</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="f0007"><img id="d1e720" src="/cms/asset/67818a79-17bc-46cb-a633-475358be858d/uaai_a_2519169_f0007_oc.jpg" loading="lazy" height="320" width="500" alt="Figure 7. Filled RAG prompt template delivered to LLM, with abbreviated context (chunks retrieved from dialogue). In this case we want to extract “Phillips Milk of Magnesia”."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="f0007" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div><p></p></li><li><p class="first last"><b>Answer Generation</b>: The complete prompt, including the retrieved context and placeholders, is then provided to the multilingual MaziyarPanahi/Llama-3-8B-Instruct-v0.8 model (temperature: 0.2). The model processes the prompt and returns the extracted information as a list.</p></li></ol><p></p><p>This method ensures that the LLM can efficiently process and extract relevant information from text, while adhering to privacy constraints and maximizing accuracy.</p><p>In step 1, we use a loop where each dialogue is divided into segments of 200 characters, with a 40-character overlap. Utilizing the LangChain library, these segments are embedded using the multilingual embedder model (intfloat/multilingual-e5-large-instruct) and indexed in a FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) vector store (Ke et al. <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="cit0016" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2024</a></span>). FAISS allows similarity search and is configured to retrieve the top k = 12 most relevant segments upon receiving the target (q_data). Before delivering the prompt to the LLM, FAISS retrieves relevant segments from its vector store based on the specified q_data value (e.g., pharmaceuticals, drugs, and medications).
